title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will connect with local pūrākau (stories) of Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors who protected these waters, and examine contemporary challenges facing our awa through oral histories, environmental reports, and community perspectives.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret oral histories of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to identify traditional kaitiakitanga practices
Create a persuasive written piece advocating for specific protection measures for our local waterway
Collaborate effectively in small groups to research and present findings on contemporary challenges facing the awa
Use appropriate te reo Māori vocabulary related to water protection and guardianship in all written and oral work

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Awa: Listening to the Water's Story
activities.description: Students will listen to recorded oral histories from local kaumātua about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, identifying key themes of protection, historical significance, and traditional practices. They will create visual mind maps connecting these stories to modern environmental concerns.

activities.title: Te Awa Inoi: River Petition Crafting
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will research current water quality data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council for our awa. Using persuasive writing techniques, they will draft formal letters or petitions to local authorities advocating for specific protection measures, incorporating both scientific data and cultural significance.

activities.title: Pūrākau Pānui: Story Analysis Circle
activities.description: Students will read and analyze traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about water guardianship, examining narrative structure, symbolic language, and cultural values. They will then work in pairs to identify modern parallels and create updated versions of these stories addressing current environmental challenges.

activities.title: Awa Ambassadors: Community Presentation
activities.description: Groups will develop and deliver short presentations to the class advocating for specific kaitiakitanga actions for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating both research findings and cultural understanding. Presentations must include appropriate visual aids and te reo Māori terminology.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Recorded oral histories from Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua (college archives)
'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' story collection by local authors
Te Reo Māori vocabulary lists for environmental protection and water management

tasks: Written persuasive letter advocating for specific protection measures for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Oral presentation demonstrating understanding of both cultural and scientific perspectives on river protection
Reflective journal documenting learning journey and personal connection to kaitiakitanga concepts

nzcAlignment: English: Processes and Strategies - Ideas; Language Features; Structure
English: Purposes and Audiences - Show understanding of how texts are shaped for different purposes and audiences
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and environments
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
